text.title: Protocols, TypedDict, and Structural Patterns
text.summary: Protocol vs ABC selection, @runtime_checkable guidance, TypedDict patterns, Literal + assert_never for exhaustive matching.

rule.statement: Use `Protocol` when the contract is structural (any class with the right methods qualifies). Use `ABC` only when a base class provides shared implementation via `super()` call chains.
rule.rationale: Protocol is "duck typing with type safety" — it enforces the contract without requiring explicit subclassing. ABC requires every implementor to import and inherit from the base class, creating coupling. Protocols are the standard for inter-module contracts.

rule.statement: Mark Protocol methods with `...` (ellipsis) bodies, not `pass` or `raise NotImplementedError`. Ellipsis is the conventional body for abstract Protocol methods.
rule.rationale: `...` is the canonical way to indicate "abstract, no body" in Protocol definitions. `raise NotImplementedError` is an ABC pattern and contradicts Protocol semantics — the Protocol itself never runs the method body.

example.note: Protocol for storage backend — no inheritance coupling
example.code:
from typing import Protocol
class StorageBackend(Protocol): def save(self, key: str, data: bytes) -> None: ... def load(self, key: str) -> bytes | None: ... def delete(self, key: str) -> bool: ...
# Both satisfy StorageBackend without importing it class S3Backend: def save(self, key: str, data: bytes) -> None: ... def load(self, key: str) -> bytes | None: ... def delete(self, key: str) -> bool: ...
class LocalBackend: def save(self, key: str, data: bytes) -> None: ... def load(self, key: str) -> bytes | None: ... def delete(self, key: str) -> bool: ...

rule.statement: Add `@runtime_checkable` only when the Protocol is used in `isinstance()` checks in production code. Omit it when the Protocol is used purely as a type annotation.
rule.rationale: `@runtime_checkable` checks only method name presence, not argument types or return types — it is weaker than a full type check. Adding it to every Protocol inflates overhead and gives a false sense of runtime safety.

rule.statement: Use TypedDict for cross-boundary dict shapes (JSON API responses, queue payloads, config dicts). Do not add TypedDict to internal dicts that are immediately converted to dataclasses or ORM models.
rule.rationale: TypedDict annotates existing dict-returning code without requiring migration to dataclasses. Adding TypedDict to a dict that is immediately `.model_validate()`-d into Pydantic creates two schemas for the same shape — one source of truth is enough.

rule.statement: Use `Literal["a", "b", "c"]` to constrain string parameters to a known set. Use `typing.assert_never()` at exhaustive match/if-elif-else branches to catch missing cases at type-check time.
rule.rationale: `Literal` lets the type checker verify that callers only pass allowed values. `assert_never()` makes match exhaustiveness a compile-time error — if a new enum variant is added, mypy reports an error at the unhandled branch, not a runtime crash.

example.note: Literal constraint and assert_never for exhaustive dispatch
example.code:
from typing import Literal, assert_never
OrderStatus = Literal["pending", "confirmed", "shipped", "cancelled"]
def next_status(current: OrderStatus) -> OrderStatus: match current: case "pending": return "confirmed" case "confirmed": return "shipped" case "shipped": return "shipped" # Terminal state case "cancelled": return "cancelled" # Terminal state case _ as unreachable: assert_never(unreachable) # mypy error if a case is unhandled
